name: Common cabbage (food medium)
basis: per 100 g fresh weight (raw green/white head cabbage, Brassica oleracea var. capitata)

components.name: D-glucose
components.evidence: Glucose is the single most abundant free sugar; glucose > fructose >> sucrose in raw cabbage.

components.name: D-fructose
components.evidence: Second most abundant free sugar.

components.name: sucrose
components.evidence: Minor disaccharide.

components.name: maltose
components.evidence: Trace.

components.name: total sugars
components.evidence: Sum ~ glucose + fructose; confirms glucose+fructose are the dominant sugars, not sucrose.

components.name: lactose
components.evidence: Absent (plant food).

components.name: galactose
components.evidence: Absent.

components.name: starch
components.evidence: Cabbage stores essentially no starch (USDA reports 0 g); the 'Starch' entry in the FooDB profile is not a real quantitative carbon source here.

components.name: dietary fiber
components.evidence: Cell-wall polysaccharide (cellulose/pectin); largely non-fermentable by most models but the main structural carbohydrate.

components.name: L-malic acid
components.source: Johanningsmeier, Fleming & Breidt 2004 J Food Sci 69(8):M222-M227 citing Fleming et al. 1988
components.evidence: DOMINANT organic acid of raw cabbage: 12.2 +/- 1.6 mM in cabbage juice (range 5-25 mM across cultivars/batches); 12.2 mM x 134.09 g/mol ~= 164 mg/100 g. THE key omission from the FooDB profile.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Aprifel white cabbage sheet; multiple HPLC organic-acid studies of Brassica oleracea
components.evidence: Present as a secondary organic acid of cabbage but not well quantified for common head cabbage; minor relative to malic acid. Included with null amount per no-fabrication rule.

components.name: oxalic acid
components.evidence: Cabbage is a genuinely low-oxalate Brassica (~4 mg/100 g); some Brassica reports up to ~50-95 mg/100 g for related crops. Low, non-dominant.

components.name: succinic acid
components.source: Sauerkraut/cabbage fermentation HPLC panels (present in fresh cabbage baseline)
components.evidence: Minor organic acid present in fresh cabbage; not reliably quantified for the raw head. Null amount.

components.name: protein (total)
components.evidence: Total N x factor; amino acid profile below is the protein-bound composition.

components.name: L-glutamate
components.evidence: Most abundant amino acid; glutamate/glutamine also dominate the FREE amino-acid pool of cabbage.

components.name: L-aspartate
components.evidence: Second most abundant amino acid.

components.name: L-phenylalanine
components.evidence: Not in FooDB profile; added.

components.name: gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA)
components.source: Cabbage GABA survey (green cabbage 3.2-7.1 mg/100 g FW)
components.evidence: Notable free non-protein amino acid; midpoint ~5 mg/100 g.

components.name: potassium
components.evidence: Major mineral.

components.name: calcium
components.evidence: Highly bioavailable (low oxalate).

components.name: fluoride
components.evidence: Trace.

components.name: vitamin C (L-ascorbate)
components.evidence: Major vitamin of cabbage.

components.name: vitamin K (phylloquinone)
components.evidence: High; leafy Brassica.

components.name: biotin (B7)
components.evidence: Present in trace amounts; not quantified by USDA. Null amount.

components.name: vitamin B12
components.evidence: Absent (plant food); microbes needing B12 must synthesize or be supplemented.

components.name: vitamin A (RAE, provitamin from carotenes)
components.evidence: Provitamin A only (from carotenoids); no preformed retinol in this plant food.

components.name: beta-carotene
components.evidence: Genuine phytochemical/provitamin A.

components.name: alpha-carotene
components.evidence: Genuine phytochemical.

components.name: linoleic acid (18:2 n-6)
components.evidence: Cabbage is very low-fat (~0.1 g total lipid); linoleic is the main PUFA.

components.name: alpha-linolenic acid (18:3 n-3)
components.evidence: Present in trace; part of the small PUFA fraction. Not separately quantified in this USDA record.

components.name: palmitic acid (16:0)
components.evidence: Main saturated fatty acid; trace overall.

components.name: oleic acid (18:1 n-9)
components.evidence: Main MUFA; trace overall.

components.name: water
components.evidence: Matrix water.

remove.name: Lycopene
remove.reason: Green/common cabbage contains no lycopene (a tomato/red-fruit carotenoid); FooDB predicted/implausible for this matrix.

remove.name: Retinol
remove.reason: Preformed retinol is animal-only; cabbage supplies vitamin A solely as provitamin-A carotenoids (alpha/beta-carotene). Implausible as a plant nutrient.

remove.name: Starch C12H20O10
remove.reason: USDA reports 0 g starch in raw cabbage; not a real quantitative carbon source (kept as 0/trace note, not a growth substrate).

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: Free cobalt(II) ion is not a defined cabbage nutrient at meaningful levels; trace contaminant-level, no growth relevance.

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: Trace element at non-nutritive levels; not a defined growth nutrient of cabbage.

remove.name: Iodine c
remove.reason: Only trace/variable levels (soil-dependent), unquantified; not a defining cabbage nutrient.

missing_added: L-malic acid (dominant organic acid, 12.2 mM; the single biggest omission)
citric acid, oxalic acid, succinic acid (organic acids, present/quantified where possible)
individual sugars with amounts: D-glucose 1.67, D-fructose 1.45, sucrose 0.08, maltose 0.01 g/100g
L-phenylalanine, L-valine (protein amino acids missing from the FooDB list)
vitamin C, K, B6, niacin, pantothenate, thiamin, riboflavin quantified; vitamin B12 flagged absent
full mineral panel with amounts (K, Ca, P, Na, Mg, Fe, Zn, Mn, Cu, Se)
keep_uncovered: quercetin, kaempferol, myricetin, isorhamnetin, apigenin, luteolin (genuine cabbage flavonoids)
cyanidin, delphinidin, peonidin, pelargonidin (anthocyanidins - genuine, mainly in red/purple cabbage, low in green)
sinigrin, glucobrassicin and other glucosinolates (genuine defining Brassica phytochemicals; no BiGG met)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 169975 (Cabbage, raw) - full nutrient panel: sugars, amino acids, fatty acids, minerals, vitamins, choline
Johanningsmeier, Fleming & Breidt 2004, J Food Sci 69(8):M222-M227 - raw cabbage malic acid 12.2 +/- 1.6 mM (5-25 mM range), citing Fleming et al. 1988
Aprifel white/green cabbage nutritional sheets - organic acid and macronutrient cross-check
Cabbage GABA survey - green cabbage 3.2-7.1 mg/100 g FW
Oxalate food-composition tables (oxalatecontent.com; Brassica oxalate reviews) - cabbage low-oxalate ~4 mg/100 g
FooDB FOOD00030 (Common cabbage) - baseline profile and phytochemical coverage
notes: Dominant carbon/energy sources for a microbe growing on cabbage are the free hexoses D-glucose (1.67 g) and D-fructose (1.45 g) plus L-malic acid (~164 mg/100 g, 12.2 mM) - malic acid was entirely absent from the FooDB profile and is the most important addition (it is the substrate for the malolactic reaction central to sauerkraut fermentation). Nitrogen is supplied mainly by glutamate/aspartate-rich protein and a glutamine/glutamate-dominated free amino-acid pool. Fat is negligible (~0.1 g/100 g). Removed lycopene and preformed retinol as implausible for this plant matrix.
